text.title: requirements-prioritization Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for requirements-prioritization. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: If all requirements are marked Must / High / Essential after elicitation, the prioritization has failed and must be re-run with a forcing function (RICE or weighted scoring that reveals relative cost and impact).
rule.rationale: Default group behavior is to mark everything as highest priority. A credible prioritization must have a distribution (roughly 60% Must, 20% Should, 20% Could for MoSCoW); a flat ranking signals incomplete elicitation or groupthink and needs a method that surfaces trade-offs.

rule.statement: Before scoring begins, the BA must lock the prioritization method and communicate the choice to all stakeholders. Switching methods mid-run is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Method-shopping (run MoSCoW, switch to RICE when results displease) destroys BA credibility. Locking the method first prevents stakeholders from manipulating the outcome.

rule.statement: Effort estimate must be gathered from delivery (engineering) before value scoring from stakeholders. Scores without effort context produce "high value, unlimited cost" results that cannot be executed.
rule.rationale: Stakeholders scoring business value with no constraint on cost estimate will rate speculative features as high-value. Coupling value scoring with effort prevents impossible scope and forces trade-off discussions.

rule.statement: Gather effort estimate from delivery (engineering) before value scoring from stakeholders. Scores without effort context produce "high value, unlimited cost" results that cannot be executed.
rule.rationale: Stakeholders scoring business value with no constraint on cost estimate will rate speculative features as high-value. Coupling value scoring with effort prevents impossible scope and forces trade-off discussions.

rule.statement: MoSCoW (Must ~60%, Should ~20%, Could ~20%, Won't = tracked for future) is best for release scope decisions with hard deadlines and clearer Must/Should boundaries. RICE is best for backlog prioritization with many items where relative trade-offs matter more than hard categories.
rule.rationale: MoSCoW works when scope is constrained and the team can clearly articulate what breaks the release if excluded. RICE works when the backlog is large and two features at similar cost but different value need relative ranking.

rule.statement: Kano (Basic/Performance/Delighters) requires user research data (survey, interviews, market analysis) to assign categories. If user research data does not exist, do not attempt Kano — treat it as gut-feel scoring dressed up in academic language and use RICE instead.
rule.rationale: Kano without data is a failure mode: the team guesses categories from the conference room, produces confident-sounding but unreliable results, and the BA gets blamed when reality diverges. RICE is more honest about uncertainty.

rule.statement: Value-Effort matrix (quadrant analysis: Quick Wins, Major Projects, Fill-Ins, Avoid) is good for visual stakeholder communication and organizational simplicity. WSJF (Weighted Shortest Job First, SAFe) is required in scaled agile contexts (ART, LeSS) where PI planning needs a method the whole organization recognizes.
rule.rationale: Value-Effort is intuitive and forces discussion ("why is this in the Avoid quadrant?"), but it loses precision for tied items. WSJF is more sophisticated (includes risk, dependencies, time-sensitivity) and aligns with SAFe ceremonies if the organization is running them.

rule.statement: "Must" means the release fails without it. "Should" means significant value but the release can work around it. "Could" means low cost, low impact. "Won't" means explicitly excluded this iteration and revisited when capacity allows.
rule.rationale: Definitions prevent scope creep. Every team will try to promote items to Must; clear operational definitions give the BA language to push back ("that's Should because we can ship without it if needed").

rule.statement: If Must > 60% of effort budget after initial assignment, the MoSCoW exercise has failed and must be re-run with a forcing function (RICE or weighted scoring) to break the tie.
rule.rationale: Default group behavior assigns everything to Must. If the math shows Must is consuming 80% of effort, the prioritization is meaningless. Forcing function (like RICE) makes relative impact visible and surfaces which "Must" items are actually "Should".

rule.statement: RICE Reach estimate must be grounded in actual user data or user research. Confidence must be 50%, 80%, or 100% with written justification per row; default 100% is a sign teams are not thinking critically about uncertainty.
rule.rationale: RICE produces confident-sounding but unreliable scores when Reach and Confidence are guesses. Forcing written justification prevents mechanical scoring and surfaces when data is actually missing.

rule.statement: For Impact, use the scale: 3 (massive, greater than 50% increase in metric), 2 (high, 25-50% increase), 1 (medium, 10-25% increase), 0.5 (low, less than 10% increase), 0.25 (minimal, less than 5% increase). Require the metric and baseline in the Impact justification.
rule.rationale: Impact estimation is the most subjective RICE component. The scale must reference a measurable baseline (retention, revenue per user, conversion) to prevent teams from calling every feature "massive" impact.

rule.statement: Kano requires user research data (survey asking "how would you feel if this feature was present / absent") to assign categories. If user research data does not exist, the team is guessing and should use RICE instead.
rule.rationale: Kano without data is gut-feel scoring. The team guesses that "dark mode" is a delighter and "login" is basic, produces confident results, then ships dark mode first and watches adoption stall. Only survey data reveals the true categorization.

rule.statement: Value-Effort works best as a visual stakeholder communication tool before moving to a more rigorous method (RICE for backlog rank, MoSCoW for release scope). Do not use it as the final prioritization artifact — it loses precision for tied items in the same quadrant.
rule.rationale: Value-Effort is intuitive ("do the Quick Wins first") but two Major Projects at (8 value, 7 effort) and (9 value, 8 effort) both land in the same quadrant with no relative ranking. Use it to frame discussion, then apply RICE for the deterministic output.

rule.statement: Lock the method before elicitation begins. The BA announces "We are using RICE for this backlog prioritization" and cannot switch mid-run even if results displease stakeholders.
rule.rationale: Method-shopping (run RICE, switch to MoSCoW when results displease) destroys credibility. Locking the method first signals the decision is process-driven, not outcome-driven. Document why you picked this method (backlog size, stakeholder count, data availability, release vs backlog).

rule.statement: Input gathering must separate elicitation into value-only (stakeholder opinion) and effort (engineering estimate). Stakeholders should NOT score effort; delivery owns that estimate. Value and effort are scored independently, then combined by the method.
rule.rationale: Mixing value and effort during elicitation produces scope inflation. Stakeholders overestimate effort, call high-effort items "low effort" to justify prioritization, or anchor engineering's estimate. Separating them forces explicit trade-off discussion.

rule.statement: Elicit individual scores before group consensus. Compute mean and standard deviation. Reconcile only rows with σ (standard deviation) > threshold (0.8 on 1-5 scale, or 20% on RICE Reach). Lock the method before scoring begins.
rule.rationale: Group scoring defaults to the loudest voice. Individual scoring then aggregation surfaces hidden disagreement (σ > 0.8 means the team fundamentally disagrees on relative importance). Reconciliation is targeted to the few high-disagreement items and documented with the rationale for the final decision.

rule.statement: Document the reconciliation outcome: "Finance scored this 4, Engineering scored 2. After discussion, agreed to 3 because [reason]: [date], [attendees]." This audit trail justifies the priority record to stakeholders who lost their item.
rule.rationale: Stakeholders ask "Why is that feature lower priority than mine?" The reconciliation record answers: "This is why we collectively valued yours at X and theirs at Y, here's the conversation that led to that." Without the record, the priority looks arbitrary.

rule.statement: Backlog prioritization is ordinal (stack rank: #1, #2, #3…). Release prioritization is categorical (MoSCoW: Must, Should, Could) or scored (RICE: rank by score). Do not mix — "Must Have, rank 23" is meaningless.
rule.rationale: The two artifacts serve different purposes: backlog answers "what do we work on next sprint?"; release answers "what scope commits do we make?" Using both on the same item creates confusion about whether the item is in or out.

rule.statement: Re-prioritization happens every sprint review (backlog) or every quarter (release). Do not set priorities at planning and forget; the world moves faster than quarterly plans.
rule.rationale: Stale priorities are a common failure: scope is locked in January, market moves, and the team ships January's priorities in March with no adjustment. Re-prioritization triggers (sprint review, or when a new high-value item arrives) keep delivery aligned to current reality.

rule.statement: If more than 60% of effort is consumed by Must/High/Critical items, the prioritization is rejected and re-run with a forcing function (RICE or weighted scoring) that forces relative ranking.
rule.rationale: Default group behavior assigns everything to the top category. A credible prioritization distributes across categories; flat distribution signals incomplete elicitation or that the team does not believe in the prioritization method.

rule.statement: Elicit individual scores before group consensus. Compute mean and standard deviation. Reconcile only rows with σ > threshold (0.8 on 1-5, or 20% on RICE Reach). Lock the method before scoring begins.
rule.rationale: These three steps prevent HiPPO, method-shopping, and guessing. Individual scoring surfaces genuine disagreement; high-σ rows get discussion; method-locking prevents the stakeholder who dislikes RICE results from switching to MoSCoW.

rule.statement: Require `req_id` (e.g., REQ-001) for every item before prioritization. Free-text items ("Export to PDF") without IDs cannot be traced to requirements docs; when scope changes, no one knows which docs to update and which stakeholder committed to the definition.
rule.rationale: Traceability is the difference between a defensible prioritization and a hand-wavy artifact. "Export to PDF" vs. "REQ-042: Users must export reports to PDF with chart/table support." The latter links to a requirement doc with acceptance criteria, estimate, owner, and stakeholder commitment.

rule.statement: Update requirement frontmatter after prioritization: priority field, priority_method, priority_setter, priority_locked_at, and reconciliation notes. This becomes the audit trail for future scope discussions.
rule.rationale: When a stakeholder asks three months later "Why isn't that feature in Q3?", the requirement doc shows the priority decision and the rationale. Without frontmatter, priorities look arbitrary.

rule.statement: Lock the method *before* elicitation. Switching methods mid-run (e.g., running MoSCoW, then switching to RICE because results displease) destroys BA credibility and creates the false record that justifies undelivered scope.
rule.rationale: Method selection is a process choice, not a result-dependent choice. Document why you picked MoSCoW over RICE (bounded scope, clear Must/Should boundaries) and stick to it even if the results surprise stakeholders. Changing methods mid-run signals that the outcome mattered more than the process, which is the beginning of the false record problem.

rule.statement: Re-prioritize monthly or every quarter. Stale priorities are worse than no priorities — they commit the team to features the market no longer values. Set a calendar reminder or tie re-prioritization to sprint reviews and quarterly planning ceremonies.
rule.rationale: Prioritization is not a one-time event; it is a standing process. Products evolve; features that were Must in Q1 may be Won't in Q3 as the market moves, competitors launch, or user research reveals different needs. Re-prioritization keeps delivery aligned to reality.

rule.statement: Validate RICE predictions quarterly: compare predicted Reach/Impact/Confidence against actual metrics (adoption, engagement, revenue) and adjust the model. If Reach predictions miss by >2x, flag it and retrain the scoring or use a simpler method.
rule.rationale: RICE scores are only as good as the data feeding them. Without feedback, teams keep making the same estimation mistakes. Quarterly review of prediction accuracy calibrates confidence levels and surfaces when the method itself is not working.

rule.statement: Agents generate artifacts (scores, ranks, reports); the BA makes three decisions: lock the method, approve reconciliation results, and sign off on final scope. Do not automate the decision-maker role.
rule.rationale: Agents are fast at mechanics (compute RICE, aggregate stakeholder scores, flag high-σ rows); humans are good at judgment (when to compromise, which stakeholder perspective matters most, escalation). The BA stays in the loop at method lock, reconciliation, and sign-off.
